forecasts.reasoning: John Morgan is well-known for teasing political runs without following through. In mid-May 2025, he firmly stated he wants to "wait to see what the field looks like" prior to deciding and emphasized that his high name recognition allows him to take much longer than other candidates to officially open a campaign account. While there is a legal deadline around June 12, 2025, for party registration (requiring candidates to be registered with their party 365 days before the June 2026 qualifying date), this only forces Morgan to update his voter registration, not to formally announce a campaign. Given his explicit strategy to delay formalizing his campaign until the field takes shape, and the very short window between the knowledge cutoff (June 6) and the resolution date (July 1), it is highly likely he will merely keep his options open and refrain from a formal announcement.

As of early June 2025, all evidence strongly indicates that neither Bulgargaz nor BOTAŞ will officially announce the cancellation of the gas agreement before July 1, 2025. The contract lacks a termination clause, and Bulgarian officials (including the Energy Minister and MPs in mid-to-late May 2025) have publicly stated that 'this agreement cannot be terminated by anybody.' Terminating it would trigger an immediate an exorbitant BGN 3-4.2 billion penalty for Bulgaria, which Bulgargaz cannot afford as it is on the verge of bankruptcy. Instead, Bulgaria is attempting to renegotiate terms by hiring a Swiss law firm and conducting ongoing talks with BOTAŞ, which offered contract amendment options in late March 2025. Neither side benefits from a unilateral explicit 'cancellation' in the span of the next 25 days, as Bulgaria wants to avoid immediate massive penalties, and BOTAŞ wants to preserve a highly lucrative long-term contract or enforce payments through arbitration if necessary. The timeline is simply too short and the structural incentives are entirely opposed to an outright cancellation announcement.

As of the knowledge cutoff in June 2025, Sudan has not ratified the AfCFTA. Sudan has been enveloped in a devastating and existential civil war between the SAF and RSF since April 2023, severely hampering the functions of the state. With no functional unified legislature to pass the agreement, and the Sovereignty Council focused on survival and military efforts, ratifying a continental trade agreement constitutes a very low priority. Given that there are merely 25 days between the knowledge cutoff (June 6, 2025) and the resolution date (July 1, 2025), and zero recent news indicating momentum toward ratification, the likelihood of this occurring in the short remaining window is exceptionally low.
